attributes.stem: A previously well 66-year-old man presents with a three month history of intermittent colicky abdominal pain and vomiting. His symptoms are worse after meals. During this time he has lost 5kg weight, which he attributes to reducing the size of his meal portions in order to avoid the onset of symptoms. He has no other gastrointestinal symptoms. He does not take any regular medication. The physical examination is unremarkable. A barium meal and follow through is arranged and the resultant imaging shown below in radiographs of the abdomen after 10 minutes (Image 1) and 1 hour (Image 2).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The contrast study shows dilated loops of a partially obstructed proximal jejunum with an apple core lesion about 30 cm distal to the duodeno-jejunal flexure (Image). This appearance is very suggestive of a neoplasm. This pattern would explain his symptoms, with are consistent with incomplete incomplete intestinal obstruction. Whilst carcinoma of the small bowel is not a common cause of small bowel obstruction, it the most likely diagnosis based on the clinical scenario and radiographic findings.

Although the patient presents with abdominal pain, a diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome is unlikely. This condition is more associated with lower digestive tracts - increasing (or decreasing pain) with defaecation, changes in stool frequency and/or changes in stool form.

Crohn disease is a reasonable diagnosis to consider, however additional symptoms such as chronic diarrhoea, gastrointestinal bleeding and extra-intestinal manifestations would be expected. Crohn disease is a risk factor for development intestinal cancer (including small bowel cancer), however, given the clinical presentation it is unlikely this preceded the development of a small intestine neoplasm. The radiological pattern of Crohn disease would more typically be segmental involvement of the small bowel and smooth walled strictures rather than the apple core appearance on this study.

An internal hernia is a potential differential which should always be considered, especially given the patient's clinical presentation as it shares clinical features including abdominal pain and postprandial nausea and vomiting. It may also present with a palpable abdominal mass and features of small bowel obstruction with subsequent strangulation. Unfortunately, internal hernias are a diagnostic dilemma, generally requiring cross sectional imaging (MRI, CT) or laparoscopic investigation. Most importantly, one would not expect the 'apple core' sign in such a pathology.

Lastly, a small bowel volvulus is another differential which displays similar symptoms to the patients presentation as well as additional symptoms related to bowel obstruction, but this is also an unlikely diagnosis. Such a presentation is more common in paediatric populations and would produce a 'cork-screw' pattern on barium meal, rather than the 'apple core' sign visible in the image. Additionally, there has also been increasing use of combined ultrasonography to aid in its diagnosis.
learningPoints: A barium meal highlighting an apple-core pattern must always prompt further investigation for intestinal neoplasia.
An internal hernia is difficult to determine clinically and should always be considered in patients presenting with abdominal pain
references: Jasti R, Carucci LR. Small bowel neoplasms: A Pictorial Review. RadioGraphics. 2020;40(4):1020–38.
Laukoetter MG, Mennigen R, Hannig CM, Osada N, Rijcken E, Vowinkel T, et al. Intestinal cancer risk in crohn’s disease: A meta-analysis. Journal of Gastrointestinal Surgery. 2010;15(4):576–83.
